blocks.text: React is a declarative, component-based JavaScript library for building user interfaces. Developed and maintained by Meta, it lets you compose complex UIs from small, isolated pieces of code called components.
What is React?

React was first released by Facebook in 2013 and quickly became one of the most popular front-end libraries in the world. At its core, React is concerned with one thing: rendering UI and reacting to changes in data. Everything else — routing, state management, data fetching — is handled by the surrounding ecosystem.

React uses a Virtual DOM to efficiently update the browser's real DOM. When your application's state changes, React calculates the minimal set of DOM mutations needed and applies only those, making updates fast and predictable.
Why Use React?

items: Component-based architecture promotes reusability and separation of concerns
Declarative style makes code easier to reason about and debug
Virtual DOM delivers high performance UI updates
Massive ecosystem with libraries for routing, state management, animations, and more
Strong community support and first-class TypeScript integration
Used by companies like Meta, Netflix, Airbnb, and thousands of startups worldwide

blocks.title: Your First React Element

blocks.text: React is a library, not a framework. It focuses solely on the view layer. For a full application you will typically combine it with tools like React Router (navigation) and a state manager such as Redux or Zustand.
Before you can build with React you need a working development environment. The fastest way to scaffold a new project is with create-react-app or the modern alternative, Vite.
Prerequisites

items: Node.js 18 or later — download from nodejs.org
npm (bundled with Node) or Yarn as a package manager
A code editor — VS Code is recommended with the ES7+ React/Redux snippets extension

blocks.text: Scaffolding with Vite (Recommended)
Vite is the modern, lightning-fast alternative to Create React App. It uses native ES modules during development, giving you near-instant hot module replacement and much faster builds.

blocks.title: Create a New React App with Vite
blocks.code:
# 1. Create the project
npm create vite@latest my-react-app -- --template react

# 2. Move into the project directory
cd my-react-app

# 3. Install dependencies
npm install

# 4. Start the development server
npm run dev
# App is now running at http://localhost:5173

blocks.text: Understanding the Project Structure

items: index.html — the single HTML page; React mounts into the #root div
src/main.jsx — entry point; creates the React root and renders <App />
src/App.jsx — root component of your application
public/ — static assets served as-is (images, fonts, etc.)
vite.config.js — Vite configuration (aliases, plugins, proxy, etc.)

blocks.text: Create React App (npx create-react-app my-app) is still widely used but is no longer actively maintained. For new projects, prefer Vite or Next.js (for server-side rendering).
JSX (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that lets you write HTML-like markup directly inside your JavaScript files. It is the heart of how React components describe their UI.
How JSX Works

JSX is not valid JavaScript on its own — it is transformed by a build tool (Babel or the Vite React plugin) into React.createElement() calls. The JSX you write is purely syntactic sugar that makes UI code more readable.

blocks.title: JSX vs createElement

blocks.text: Key JSX Rules

items: A component must return a single root element — wrap siblings in a <div> or a Fragment (<>...</>)
Use className instead of class, and htmlFor instead of for
All tags must be closed — self-closing tags need a slash: <img />, <input />
JavaScript expressions go inside curly braces: {2 + 2}, {user.name}
Inline styles are objects with camelCase properties: style={{ backgroundColor: 'red' }}

blocks.title: JSX Expressions and Fragments

blocks.text: JSX comments use {/* ... */} syntax inside the markup. Regular // single-line comments work only in JavaScript expressions, not directly between JSX tags.
Components are the building blocks of every React application. A component is a reusable, self-contained piece of UI that manages its own structure, style, and behaviour.
Function Components
Modern React uses function components — plain JavaScript functions that accept a props object and return JSX. Class components are legacy; you will rarely write them in new code.

blocks.title: Defining and Using a Function Component

blocks.text: Component Naming and File Conventions

items: Component names must start with a capital letter — MyButton not myButton
One component per file is the standard convention
File names typically match the component name: UserCard.jsx
Use export default for the primary component of a file
Use named exports when a file contains multiple utility components

blocks.title: Composing Components

blocks.text: Rendering Lists of Components

blocks.title: Rendering a List with .map()

blocks.text: Always provide a stable, unique key prop when rendering lists. React uses keys to efficiently reconcile the Virtual DOM. Avoid using the array index as a key when the list can be reordered or filtered.
Props (short for properties) are the mechanism React uses to pass data from a parent component down to a child component. They make components reusable and configurable.
Passing and Reading Props

Props are passed as HTML-like attributes on a component tag and received inside the component as a plain JavaScript object. You can destructure props directly in the function signature for cleaner code.

blocks.title: Passing and Destructuring Props

blocks.text: Default Props
You can specify default values for props using default parameter syntax directly in the function signature. This keeps components resilient when a parent omits an optional prop.

blocks.title: Default Prop Values

blocks.text: The children Prop
Every React component automatically receives a special children prop that contains whatever JSX is nested between its opening and closing tags. This is how you build container or wrapper components.

blocks.title: Using the children Prop

blocks.text: Props are read-only. A component must never modify its own props. Data flows in one direction — from parent to child — which makes React apps easier to trace and debug.
State is data that can change over time and cause a component to re-render. The useState hook is the primary way to add reactive state to a function component.
The useState Hook

useState is a React Hook that lets you declare a state variable. It returns an array with two elements: the current state value and a setter function that you call to update it. When the setter is called, React re-renders the component with the new value.

blocks.title: Counter with useState

blocks.text: State with Objects and Arrays

State can hold any JavaScript value — strings, numbers, booleans, objects, and arrays. When updating objects or arrays you must replace them with a new value rather than mutating in place, because React uses reference equality to detect changes.

blocks.title: Managing Object and Array State

blocks.text: Rules of Hooks

items: Only call hooks at the top level of a function component — never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions
Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks — not from regular JavaScript functions
Hook names must start with use by convention (e.g., useState, useEffect, useMyCustomHook)

blocks.text: State updates in React are asynchronous. If you need the next state to depend on the previous state, pass a function to the setter: setCount(prev => prev + 1). This guarantees you are working with the latest value.
React makes it straightforward to respond to user interactions like clicks, key presses, and form submissions by attaching event handlers directly in JSX.
Attaching Event Handlers

React event handlers are camelCase attributes (e.g., onClick, onChange, onSubmit) and receive a function reference — not a function call. React wraps native browser events in its own SyntheticEvent object that normalises behaviour across all browsers.

blocks.title: Click and Change Events

blocks.text: Handling Form Submission

blocks.title: Controlled Form with onSubmit

blocks.text: Passing Arguments to Handlers
When you need to pass extra data to an event handler — such as an item's ID in a list — wrap the call in an arrow function inside the JSX attribute. This defers execution until the event fires.

blocks.title: Passing Arguments via Arrow Functions

blocks.text: Common React event names: onClick, onDoubleClick, onChange, onInput, onSubmit, onKeyDown, onKeyUp, onFocus, onBlur, onMouseEnter, onMouseLeave. All follow the camelCase convention.

Conditional rendering in React means showing different UI based on certain conditions — just like if statements in regular JavaScript. React lets you use JavaScript expressions like if/else, the ternary operator ? :, and the logical && operator directly inside JSX to control what gets rendered.
Using if/else Outside JSX

The simplest approach is to use a standard if/else block before the return statement. You can assign JSX to a variable and render it conditionally. This keeps the JSX clean and readable when the logic is complex.

blocks.title: if/else conditional rendering

blocks.text: Ternary Operator and && Shorthand

blocks.title: Ternary operator and logical && inside JSX

items: if/else — best for complex multi-branch logic before the return
Ternary condition ? a : b — best for inline two-way choices inside JSX
&& shorthand — best for optionally rendering a single element
Nullish coalescing ?? — useful for providing fallback values
Extract complex conditions into helper functions to keep JSX readable

blocks.text: Be careful with the && operator when the left side could be 0. In JavaScript, 0 && <X /> renders the number 0 on screen. Always ensure the left side evaluates to a proper boolean, e.g. count > 0 && <X />.

Rendering lists is one of the most common tasks in React. You use JavaScript's Array.map() method to transform an array of data into an array of JSX elements. React requires each element in a list to have a unique key prop so it can efficiently update the DOM when the list changes.
Rendering a Basic List with map()

blocks.title: Rendering a list of items

blocks.text: Why Keys Matter

Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or removed. React uses keys as hints to reconcile the virtual DOM efficiently. Without keys (or with unstable keys like array indices), React may re-render more elements than necessary or produce subtle UI bugs when the list order changes.

blocks.title: Rendering a list of components with keys

items: Keys must be unique among siblings, not globally unique
Prefer stable IDs (database IDs, UUIDs) over array indices as keys
Array index as key is acceptable only for static, never-reordered lists
Keys are not accessible as props inside the child component — pass ID separately if needed
React throws a warning in development when keys are missing

blocks.text: Never use Math.random() as a key. It generates a new value on every render, forcing React to unmount and remount every list item, destroying component state and degrading performance.

In React, form elements like <input>, <textarea>, and <select> can be controlled — meaning React state is the single source of truth for their values. This gives you complete control over validation, formatting, and submission logic.
Controlled vs Uncontrolled Inputs

An uncontrolled input manages its own state in the DOM — you read its value via a ref when needed. A controlled input binds its value prop to React state and updates via onChange. Controlled inputs are the recommended pattern because they make form data predictable and easy to validate in real time.

blocks.title: Controlled input with useState

blocks.text: Handling Multiple Inputs with One Handler

blocks.title: Generic onChange handler using input name

items: Always call e.preventDefault() in onSubmit to stop the default browser page reload
Use htmlFor in JSX instead of the HTML for attribute on labels
For checkboxes, read e.target.checked instead of e.target.value
Use the name attribute with a single handler to manage many inputs via one state object
Consider libraries like React Hook Form or Formik for large, complex forms with validation

blocks.text: Setting value without an onChange handler creates a read-only input and triggers a React warning. If you intentionally want a default that the user can change without React controlling it, use defaultValue instead.

useEffect is React's way of synchronising a component with an external system — fetching data, subscribing to events, updating the document title, or setting up timers. It runs after the component renders, keeping side effects out of the pure rendering logic.
Anatomy of useEffect

useEffect accepts two arguments: a setup function containing the side-effect logic, and an optional dependency array. React re-runs the effect whenever the values in the dependency array change. If the array is empty ([]), the effect runs only once after the initial render — equivalent to componentDidMount in class components.

blocks.title: Fetching data with useEffect

blocks.text: Cleanup Functions

blocks.title: useEffect with event listener cleanup

items: No dependency array — runs after every render
Empty array [] — runs once after the initial render
Array with values [a, b] — runs when a or b changes
Return a cleanup function to unsubscribe, clear timers, or cancel requests
Do not set state unconditionally without a dependency array — it causes infinite loops

blocks.text: In React 18 Strict Mode, effects run twice in development to help surface cleanup bugs. Always write a proper cleanup function — your code should be resilient to the effect being mounted, unmounted, and remounted.

useRef returns a mutable ref object whose .current property persists across renders without triggering a re-render when changed. It is most commonly used to access DOM nodes directly or to store a mutable value that you want to remember between renders without causing the component to re-render.
Accessing DOM Elements

Pass a ref created with useRef to a JSX element's ref attribute. After the component mounts, ref.current points to the real DOM node. This is useful for programmatic focus management, measuring element dimensions, or integrating with third-party DOM libraries.

blocks.title: Focus an input programmatically with useRef

blocks.text: Storing Mutable Values Across Renders

blocks.title: useRef to track previous value and store a timer ID

items: Mutating ref.current does not trigger a re-render — use state for values that should update the UI
The ref object is the same instance across all renders — useRef(initialValue) only sets the initial value once
Use refs to hold timer IDs, subscription handles, or animation frame IDs that need cleanup
Prefer useRef over instance variables when working in functional components
Do not read or write ref.current during rendering — do it inside effects or event handlers

blocks.text: useRef is different from createRef. createRef creates a new ref object on every render and is intended for class components. Always use useRef inside functional components.

Custom hooks let you extract and reuse stateful logic across multiple components. A custom hook is simply a JavaScript function whose name starts with use and that calls one or more React hooks internally. They are the idiomatic React pattern for sharing logic without changing the component hierarchy.
Why Custom Hooks?

Before hooks, sharing stateful logic required patterns like higher-order components or render props, which added extra layers to the component tree. Custom hooks solve the same problem but in a simpler, flat way — the logic lives in a plain function, not in a wrapper component. Any two components using the same custom hook get independent copies of its state.

blocks.title: useLocalStorage — persist state in localStorage

blocks.text: useFetch — Data Fetching Hook

blocks.title: useFetch — reusable data fetching with loading and error states

items: Custom hook names must start with use — this is how React identifies them and enforces the rules of hooks
Each component that calls a custom hook gets its own isolated state — hooks do not share state between callers
Custom hooks can call other custom hooks, enabling composable layers of logic
Return whatever the consumer needs — a value, an array like useState, or an object like { data, loading, error }
Test custom hooks independently using the @testing-library/react-hooks or Vitest utilities

blocks.text: If you find yourself copy-pasting the same useState + useEffect combination across multiple components, that is a strong signal to extract a custom hook. Common candidates include window size tracking, debouncing, media query detection, and form validation logic.

The Context API is React's built-in solution for sharing data across a component tree without passing props at every level. By combining createContext, a Provider, and the useContext hook you can make any value globally accessible to any descendant component.
The Problem: Prop Drilling

Prop drilling occurs when data must be passed through several intermediate components that don't actually use it — just to reach a deeply nested child. This clutters components with unnecessary props and makes refactoring painful. Context solves this by letting you teleport a value directly to the component that needs it.
Creating and Providing Context

blocks.title: ThemeContext — createContext, Provider, and custom hook
Consuming context with useContext

items: createContext(defaultValue) — creates a context object; default value is used when no Provider is found above
Provider value={...} — makes data available to all descendants; re-renders consumers when value changes
useContext(MyContext) — reads the nearest Provider's value inside any functional component
Wrap the custom hook in a guard to produce clear error messages when used outside the Provider
Multiple independent contexts can coexist; nest Providers as needed

blocks.text: Context is great for low-frequency updates (theme, locale, auth user). For high-frequency updates (e.g., every keystroke) consider pairing it with useMemo or reaching for a dedicated state library like Zustand or Redux Toolkit to avoid unnecessary re-renders.

useReducer is an alternative to useState for managing complex state logic. It follows the same reducer pattern popularised by Redux: you dispatch actions and a pure reducer function decides how state transitions, keeping every update predictable and easy to test.
useState vs useReducer

Use useState for simple, independent values. Switch to useReducer when: state is an object with multiple related fields, the next state depends on the previous state in non-trivial ways, or multiple actions can modify the same piece of state. The reducer keeps all that logic in one place.
Anatomy of useReducer

blocks.title: Counter with useReducer
Form state managed with useReducer

items: useReducer(reducer, initialState) returns [state, dispatch]
The reducer must be a pure function — no side effects, always return a new state object
Actions are plain objects; by convention they have a type string and optional payload
Dispatching the same action with the same state always produces the same result — easy to unit test
Combine with Context to share dispatch and state across a component tree (a lightweight Redux)

blocks.text: Always return a new state object from the reducer rather than mutating the existing one. React uses shallow comparison to detect changes, so mutating state directly will prevent re-renders.

useMemo and useCallback are performance hooks that memoize values and functions across renders. They tell React to skip recomputing an expensive value or recreating a function reference unless specific dependencies change.
useMemo — Memoizing Computed Values

blocks.title: useMemo to cache an expensive calculation

blocks.text: useCallback — Memoizing Functions

Every render creates a new function instance. When you pass a callback to a child component wrapped in React.memo, a new function reference triggers an unnecessary re-render. useCallback returns a stable reference that only changes when the listed dependencies change.

blocks.title: useCallback with React.memo to prevent child re-renders

items: useMemo(() => compute(), [deps]) — returns a memoized value
useCallback(fn, [deps]) — returns a memoized function reference
Both only rerun when a dependency in the array changes between renders
Pair useCallback with React.memo on child components for maximum benefit
Don't over-memoize — the overhead of memoization itself is not free; apply it to genuinely expensive operations

blocks.text: A common rule of thumb: reach for useMemo/useCallback when you can measure a performance problem with the React DevTools Profiler. Premature memoization can make code harder to read without meaningful gains.

React Router v6 is the de-facto standard for client-side routing in React applications. It maps URL paths to components, supports nested layouts, dynamic segments, and programmatic navigation — all without a full-page reload.
Setting Up Routes

blocks.title: Basic routing with BrowserRouter, Routes, and Route

blocks.text: Dynamic Segments and Nested Routes

blocks.title: useParams for dynamic segments and nested layouts

blocks.text: Use the useNavigate hook for programmatic navigation (e.g., after form submission). navigate('/dashboard') pushes a new entry; navigate(-1) goes back like the browser's back button.

items: <BrowserRouter> — wraps the app at the root level to enable routing
<Routes> / <Route> — declaratively maps paths to component elements
<Link to> — renders an accessible anchor that updates the URL without reload
useParams() — reads dynamic URL segments like :userId
useNavigate() — imperative navigation from event handlers
<Outlet /> — placeholder inside a layout route where child routes render

blocks.text: In React Router v6 the Switch component from v5 is replaced by Routes, and route matching is now always exact by default. If you're upgrading from v5, review the official migration guide.

Most React applications need to load data from an API. The browser's built-in Fetch API combined with useEffect and useState is the foundational pattern. For production apps, libraries like React Query (TanStack Query) add caching, refetching, and loading/error state management on top.
Basic fetch with useEffect

blocks.title: Fetching a list with loading and error states

blocks.text: Custom useFetch Hook

blocks.title: Reusable useFetch hook

items: Always handle loading, error, and success states for a good UX
Use AbortController to cancel in-flight requests when a component unmounts
Check res.ok before parsing JSON — a 4xx/5xx response does not throw by default
A custom hook (e.g. useFetch) removes boilerplate and keeps components clean
For complex scenarios consider TanStack Query (React Query) for caching, pagination, and background refetching

blocks.text: Never update state on an unmounted component. The AbortController cleanup pattern in the useEffect return function prevents this and also avoids memory leaks in strict mode where effects run twice in development.

Error Boundaries are React class components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree, log them, and display a fallback UI instead of crashing the whole application. They are the React equivalent of a try/catch block for the render phase.
Creating an Error Boundary

blocks.title: Class-based ErrorBoundary component

blocks.text: Using Error Boundaries

blocks.title: Wrapping feature sections with ErrorBoundary

items: Error boundaries must be class components — there is no hook equivalent yet (as of React 18)
getDerivedStateFromError — updates state to show the fallback UI (runs during render)
componentDidCatch — ideal for logging errors to an external service (runs after render)
Wrap different sections of your app in separate boundaries so one failure doesn't take everything down
Error boundaries do not catch errors in event handlers — use regular try/catch there

blocks.text: The popular library react-error-boundary provides a hook-friendly API (useErrorBoundary) and a flexible ErrorBoundary component with onReset support, saving you from writing the class component yourself.

React Portals let you render a child component into a DOM node that exists outside the parent component's DOM hierarchy. This is the go-to technique for modals, tooltips, and dropdown menus that must visually escape an ancestor's overflow: hidden or z-index stacking context.
Creating a Portal

ReactDOM.createPortal(children, domNode) accepts JSX and a target DOM element. The rendered output appears at domNode in the real DOM, but React event bubbling and context still flow through the React component tree, not the DOM tree — so events bubble up through the React parent as expected.

blocks.title: Modal component using createPortal
Triggering the Modal from any component

items: Add <div id="modal-root"></div> to index.html alongside #root
React context and event bubbling still work through the logical React tree, not the DOM tree
Useful for: modals, drawers, tooltips, toasts, and dropdown menus
Remember to manage focus and keyboard accessibility (ARIA roles, focus trapping) inside modals

blocks.text: A click event fired inside a portal will still bubble up to React ancestors of the portal's mounting point in the React component tree. This is intentional and allows parent components to handle events from portal children naturally.

React is fast by default, but large applications can develop performance bottlenecks — unnecessary re-renders, expensive computations, and large bundle sizes. React provides several tools to address these: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, lazy loading, and the React DevTools Profiler.
React.memo — Skipping Re-renders

blocks.title: React.memo to prevent unnecessary child renders

blocks.text: Code Splitting with React.lazy

blocks.title: Lazy loading routes to reduce initial bundle size

items: React.memo(Component) — shallow-compares props; skips re-render if unchanged
useMemo / useCallback — memoize expensive values and stable function references
React.lazy + Suspense — split bundles per route or feature, reducing initial load time
Virtualise long lists with react-window or react-virtual to render only visible rows
Use the React DevTools Profiler to identify which components render most often and why

blocks.text: Profile before optimising. The React DevTools Profiler (browser extension) records component render times and highlights wasted renders. Optimise only the components shown to be slow — over-memoising everything adds complexity without measurable benefit.

Adding TypeScript to React gives you compile-time type safety, better IDE autocompletion, and self-documenting component APIs. Vite and Create React App both support TypeScript out of the box — just select the TS template when scaffolding.
Typing Props and State

blocks.title: Typed functional component with interface props

blocks.text: Typing Hooks and Events

blocks.title: Typed useReducer, useRef, and event handlers

items: Use interface or type to define component prop shapes — prefer interface for extensibility
React.FC<Props> is optional; typing the destructured parameter directly is more concise
useState<T>(initial) — add a generic when TypeScript cannot infer the type from the initial value
Event types: ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>, FormEvent<HTMLFormElement>, MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>
useRef<HTMLElement>(null) — type the ref to the specific DOM element for safe property access

blocks.text: Install the React type definitions with npm i -D @types/react @types/react-dom if you're not using a TypeScript template. The Vite command npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts sets everything up automatically.

Testing gives you confidence that your components work as users expect. The most widely used tools in the React ecosystem are Vitest (or Jest) as the test runner, React Testing Library (RTL) for rendering components in a test environment, and @testing-library/user-event for simulating realistic user interactions.
Testing Library Philosophy

RTL encourages you to test components the way users interact with them — by querying for visible text, roles, and labels rather than implementation details like class names or internal state. The guiding principle is: "The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they give you."

blocks.title: Unit test for a Counter component
Testing async data fetching with MSW mocks

items: Query priority: getByRole > getByLabelText > getByText > getByTestId
Use userEvent over fireEvent — it simulates the full browser event sequence
waitFor and findBy* queries handle async updates (data fetching, animations)
MSW (Mock Service Worker) intercepts real fetch/XHR calls — no need to mock modules
Aim for mostly integration tests (component + children) with a few unit tests for complex pure functions

blocks.text: Avoid testing implementation details such as internal state values or private function calls. If a refactor breaks your tests without changing user-visible behaviour, your tests are testing the wrong things.

As React applications grow, recurring problems emerge around code reuse, component composition, and separation of concerns. Design patterns are proven solutions to these problems. The most important ones in the React world are: Custom Hooks, Compound Components, Render Props, and the Container/Presentational split.
Custom Hooks — Reusable Logic

blocks.title: useLocalStorage custom hook

blocks.text: Compound Components — Flexible Composition

blocks.title: Compound Tabs component

items: Custom Hooks — extract stateful logic into reusable functions prefixed with use
Compound Components — split a complex component into co-operating sub-components that share implicit state via Context
Render Props — pass a function as a prop to share logic; largely superseded by hooks but still useful
Container / Presentational — separate data-fetching logic from UI rendering for easier testing
Higher-Order Components (HOC) — wrap a component to inject behaviour; use hooks instead when possible

blocks.text: Prefer Custom Hooks over Render Props and HOCs for sharing logic in modern React. They are simpler, compose better, and don't add extra nodes to the component tree.

Congratulations on completing the React course! React itself is just the foundation — the real-world ecosystem around it gives you everything you need to build fast, scalable, production-ready applications. Here is a curated map of the most important tools and where to go next.
Meta-Frameworks

Next.js is the most popular React meta-framework. It adds server-side rendering (SSR), static site generation (SSG), the App Router with React Server Components, API routes, image optimisation, and more — all with zero-config defaults. Remix is a great alternative that focuses on web standards and progressive enhancement. For pure static sites, Gatsby remains an option.
Ecosystem at a Glance

blocks.title: A modern React stack example

items: Next.js — SSR, SSG, RSC, file-based routing, and API routes out of the box
TanStack Query — server state management with caching, background sync, and pagination
Zustand / Jotai / Redux Toolkit — client-side global state (choose based on complexity)
React Hook Form + Zod — performant forms with schema validation
Tailwind CSS — utility-first CSS that pairs naturally with React components
Vitest + Testing Library + MSW — fast, modern testing stack
Storybook — develop and document UI components in isolation

blocks.text: The best next step is to build something real. Pick a project idea, scaffold it with create-next-app, and gradually introduce libraries as you need them. The React and Next.js official docs (react.dev and nextjs.org/docs) are excellent and kept up to date — make them your primary reference.
